core.models.energy_measurement module

ENERGY MEASUREMENT DATA MODELS

This module defines the data structures used throughout Module 1 to represent energy measurements, performance counters, and hardware state.

All data classes are designed to be: - Self-documenting (field names explain their purpose) - Serializable (can be saved to JSON/database) - Type-safe (using dataclasses with type hints)

Author: Deepak Panigrahy

class core.models.energy_measurement.EnergyMeasurement(measurement_id: str, start_time: float, end_time: float, duration_seconds: float, package_energy_uj: int = 0, core_energy_uj: int = 0, dram_energy_uj: int | None = None, gpu_energy_uj: int | None = None, uncore_waste_uj: int = 0, idle_baseline_energy_uj: float = 0.0, performance_counters: PerformanceCounters = <factory>, thermal: ThermalReadings = <factory>, power_state: PowerState = <factory>, run_queue_length: float = 0.0, kernel_time_ms: float = 0.0, user_time_ms: float = 0.0, sample_count: int = 0, sampling_rate_hz: float = 0.0, measurement_valid: bool = True, validation_issues: List[str] = <factory>, raw_rapl_start: Dict[str, int] | None=None, raw_rapl_end: Dict[str, int] | None=None)[source]

Bases: object

Complete energy measurement result from a single experiment run.

This is the primary output of Module 1. It contains everything needed for sustainability calculations (Module 2) and orchestration tax analysis (Module 3).

The object is designed to be: - Self-contained (all data in one place) - Serializable (can be saved to database) - Comparable (can compare linear vs agentic runs)

Req 1.1 through 1.47 are all represented here.

calculate_derived_metrics()[source]

Calculate derived metrics from raw readings.

This should be called after populating raw data.

core_energy_uj: int = 0

Core-only energy in microjoules.

dram_energy_uj: int | None = None

DRAM energy in microjoules (may be None if unavailable).

duration_seconds: float

Total duration of measurement in seconds.

end_time: float

Unix timestamp when measurement ended.

gpu_energy_uj: int | None = None

GPU energy in microjoules (may be None if unavailable).

idle_baseline_energy_uj: float = 0.0

Energy the system would consume if completely idle (subtracted).

inference_ratio() → float[source]

Estimate ratio of energy used for actual inference vs. overhead.

This is a heuristic based on uncore waste and core activity.

Req 2.11: Reasoning-to-waste ratio

Returns:

Ratio between 0.0 and 1.0

Return type:

float

kernel_time_ms: float = 0.0

Time spent in kernel mode (milliseconds).

measurement_id: str

Unique identifier for this measurement (e.g., ‘meas_20260220_143022’).

measurement_valid: bool = True

Whether the measurement passed validation checks.

package_energy_uj: int = 0

Total package energy in microjoules (PKG domain).

performance_counters: PerformanceCounters

Hardware performance counter readings.

power_state: PowerState

CPU power state information.

raw_rapl_end: Dict[str, int] | None = None

Raw RAPL readings at end (for debugging).

raw_rapl_start: Dict[str, int] | None = None

Raw RAPL readings at start (for debugging).

run_queue_length: float = 0.0

Average number of processes in run queue (from /proc/loadavg).

sample_count: int = 0

Number of RAPL samples taken during measurement.

sampling_rate_hz: float = 0.0

Actual sampling rate achieved (samples / second).

start_time: float

Unix timestamp when measurement started.

thermal: ThermalReadings

Temperature sensor readings.

to_dict() → Dict[str, Any][source]

Convert to dictionary for serialization.

Returns:

Dict ready for JSON serialization

to_json() → str[source]

Convert to JSON string for saving to file.

Returns:

JSON string with pretty formatting

uncore_waste_uj: int = 0

Energy consumed by uncore (cache, memory controller, interconnect). Calculated as: package - core - dram - gpu

Req 1.3: This is the energy attributed to orchestration overhead.

user_time_ms: float = 0.0

Time spent in user mode (milliseconds).

validation_issues: List[str]

List of issues found during validation (if any).

class core.models.energy_measurement.PerformanceCounters(instructions_retired: int = 0, cpu_cycles: int = 0, cache_references: int = 0, cache_misses: int = 0, major_page_faults: int = 0, minor_page_faults: int = 0, context_switches_voluntary: int = 0, context_switches_involuntary: int = 0, thread_migrations: int = 0, branches: int = 0, branch_misses: int = 0, cpu_clock_ms: float = 0.0, task_clock_ms: float = 0.0)[source]

Bases: object

Hardware performance counter readings from perf_events.

These counters provide insight into what the CPU was actually doing during the measurement interval. They help distinguish between: - Actual computation (instructions retired) - Memory bottlenecks (cache misses) - OS scheduler overhead (context switches)

Req 1.5: Instruction density (IPC) Req 1.6: Cache behavior Req 1.10: Page faults Req 1.43: Thread migrations

branch_misses: int = 0

Mispredicted branches.

branches: int = 0

Branch instructions executed.

cache_miss_rate() → float[source]

Calculate cache miss rate.

Higher miss rates indicate memory bandwidth pressure, which can increase energy consumption significantly.

Req 1.6: Cache behavior

Returns:

Miss rate between 0.0 and 1.0

Return type:

float

cache_misses: int = 0

Number of cache misses (LLC misses).

cache_references: int = 0

Number of cache references (LLC accesses).

context_switches_involuntary: int = 0

Involuntary context switches (scheduler preempted).

context_switches_voluntary: int = 0

Voluntary context switches (process yielded CPU).

cpu_clock_ms: float = 0.0

CPU time consumed in milliseconds.

cpu_cycles: int = 0

Number of CPU cycles elapsed.

instructions_per_cycle() → float[source]

Calculate Instructions Per Cycle (IPC).

IPC tells us how efficiently the CPU was running: - High IPC (>1) means CPU was doing useful work - Low IPC (<0.5) suggests stalls (waiting for memory)

Req 1.5: Instruction density

Returns:

Instructions per cycle, or 0.0 if no cycles

Return type:

float

instructions_retired: int = 0

Number of instructions retired (actual work done).

major_page_faults: int = 0

Major page faults (needed disk I/O).

minor_page_faults: int = 0

Minor page faults (resolved in memory).

task_clock_ms: float = 0.0

Task elapsed time in milliseconds.

thread_migrations: int = 0

Thread migrations between CPU cores.

to_dict() → Dict[str, Any][source]

Convert to dictionary for serialization.

Returns:

Dict with all fields and derived metrics

total_context_switches() → int[source]

Total context switches (voluntary + involuntary).

Req 1.12: Kernel context switches

Returns:

Total number of context switches

Return type:

int

class core.models.energy_measurement.PowerState(frequencies_mhz: Dict[int, float]=<factory>, tsc_frequency_mhz: float = 0.0, c_state_residencies: Dict[str, float]=<factory>, igpu_rc6_percent: float = 0.0, igpu_frequency_mhz: float = 0.0, package_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0, package_power_watts: float = 0.0, core_power_watts: float = 0.0, gpu_power_watts: float = 0.0, ram_power_watts: float = 0.0)[source]

Bases: object

CPU power state information including frequencies and C-states.

Modern CPUs save power by entering deeper sleep states (C-states) and lower frequencies (P-states). This data helps attribute energy to actual work vs. waiting.

Req 1.4: DVFS attribution (frequency) Req 1.7: C-state residency Req 1.8: iGPU states Req 1.41: Core-specific C-states

c_state_residencies: Dict[str, float]

Time spent in each C-state as percentage. Keys: ‘C1’, ‘C1E’, ‘C3’, ‘C6’, ‘C7’, ‘C8’, ‘C9’, ‘C10’

core_power_watts: float = 0.0

Core power estimate in watts.

deepest_cstate() → str | None[source]

Identify the deepest C-state entered.

Req 1.41: Deepest core C-state

Returns:

Name of deepest C-state (e.g., ‘C7’) or None

Return type:

str

frequencies_mhz: Dict[int, float]

Current frequency per core in MHz.

gpu_power_watts: float = 0.0

GPU power estimate in watts.

igpu_frequency_mhz: float = 0.0

Integrated GPU frequency in MHz.

igpu_rc6_percent: float = 0.0

Render C6 state residency for iGPU (power saving).

package_power_watts: float = 0.0

Package power estimate in watts.

package_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0

CPU package temperature in Celsius.

ram_power_watts: float = 0.0

DRAM power estimate in watts.

to_dict() → Dict[str, Any][source]

Convert to dictionary for serialization.

Returns:

Dict with all power state data

tsc_frequency_mhz: float = 0.0

Time Stamp Counter frequency in MHz (constant).

class core.models.energy_measurement.ThermalReadings(package_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0, core_temperatures_celsius: List[float] = <factory>, gpu_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0, pch_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0, thermal_throttle_count: int = 0, prochot_events: int = 0)[source]

Bases: object

Temperature sensor readings from various thermal zones.

Temperature is critical for: - Detecting thermal throttling (Req 1.9) - Ensuring measurements aren’t skewed by overheating - Validating cooldown periods between runs

Req 1.9: Package thermal jitter

core_temperatures_celsius: List[float]

Per-core temperatures (may vary significantly).

gpu_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0

Integrated GPU temperature if available.

is_throttling(threshold: float = 85.0) → bool[source]

Check if CPU is thermal throttling.

Most CPUs throttle around 85-100°C. If we’re above threshold, measurements may be unreliable.

Parameters:

threshold – Temperature threshold in Celsius

Returns:

True if temperature exceeds threshold

Return type:

bool

package_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0

CPU package temperature in Celsius.

pch_temperature_celsius: float = 0.0

Platform Controller Hub temperature.

prochot_events: int = 0

PROCHOT (processor hot) assertion count.

thermal_throttle_count: int = 0

Number of thermal throttling events during measurement.

to_dict() → Dict[str, Any][source]

Convert to dictionary for serialization.

Returns:

Dict with all thermal data
